Hey guys, Saturday, time for your extra food for thought motivation. And again, these are clips that I'm going to do research for each week and find yourself. I think some of them really are the great moments where I was on stage, said things that really didn't have to do that much with selling, more about life in general, food for thought. Now if you're selling out there in the field today, it's Saturday, right? Hey, well, guess what? You know, make sure you go back and listen to a daily motivation from another day, right? Get yourself pumped up. But this is more just for you personally to kind of crystallize what success is about, what life is about, and how you can feel good about yourself. So check out this clip. You're going to enjoy it. And I'm not going to see you on the other side. It's going to go right to the ending. My gift to you. My parents were completely called risk averse. They were not taking any risks in life. They were from that oppression era mentality, where they hoarded their money, they didn't invest at all, and that's really cool, playing defense your whole life. They never played offense. And it's very hard to get ahead, especially in selling to salesperson, playing defense your whole life. So my parents never had any money. So when I went out to the world realizing that, and I was frustrated, and didn't have any money, and I still like that I'm struggling. So I had these high financial standards. I wanted to go to college. So rather than just taking the money and partying for a day, the next day I went back to four pullers, and I sold them all out and made $500 the next day. That was a lot of money in 1978, $500 in a day in cash. That first summer I made $20,000. My first summer selling ISIS. I then did that every single summer, and I then used that money to put myself through school, put myself through college that way. And I graduated college, and before I went out into the business world and staked my claim, I actually had one pick up, so to speak, on the road to entrepreneurial to sales success. And that was, I spent one day in dental school. Believe it or not, I ain't dentists in the room. I want to be a dentist. I mean, honestly, it's the worst profession. I hope this is on dentists' hip, by the way. But I mean, you got to stick your hands in people's mouths and just gross, right? Here's what happened. From the time I was 18 months old, and I was in the high chair, and my mom was giving me the bottle of the paparazzi in the applesauce, from that first moment, as the food was going in, she was saying the only way to become wealthy is you got to be a doctor. You're a Jewish mother. You got to be a doctor. You're a normal wealth doctor. It's like, okay, and it basically is, right? Hungry food, doctor, right? So what my mother was doing, just so you know, she was inserting a belief in me. And that makes up the second element of this intergame of success, the beliefs that we have.
